mnemonics.title: F-R-M: Front Rear Modulo
mnemonics.content: **F**ront and **R**ear both use **M**odulo. For enqueue: rear = (rear + 1) % capacity. For dequeue: front = (front + 1) % capacity. The modulo operation ensures both pointers wrap around seamlessly.

mnemonics.title: Full Check: Rear+1 Circles to Front
mnemonics.content: Queue is FULL when the slot immediately after Rear (wrapping around) equals Front. Formula: (rear + 1) % capacity == front. Think: 'Rear is chasing Front — when it catches up, the queue is full!'

mnemonics.title: Empty: Both at -1
mnemonics.content: Queue is EMPTY when front == -1 AND rear == -1. Both pointers start at -1 and return to -1 after the last element is dequeued.

mnemonics.title: Clock Wrapping Analogy
mnemonics.content: Just like clock hands wrap from 12 back to 1, queue indices wrap from N-1 back to 0 using modulo. After index 4 comes index 0 in a size-5 queue: (4+1)%5 = 0.

mnemonics.title: One Slot Sacrifice Rule
mnemonics.content: Array of size N stores only N-1 elements. The sacrificed slot prevents 'full vs. empty' ambiguity — without it, front == rear could mean either state.

mnemonics.title: First Enqueue: Both to 0
mnemonics.content: When adding the FIRST element to an empty queue: set front = 0 AND rear = 0. Don't use circular increment — directly assign both to 0.
